PERIOD COVERED: February 1 - March 1, 1975

STEN PROCESSING

Detailed drawings of the stem line modifications have been com-
pleted, and the installation job should be going out for bid
shortly. To have the capability of casing stem after expansion
and feeding it directly into the blend line, we need to design

and fabricate a cylinder and spray system to handle tobacco Flow
rates of ¥10-30 1b/hr. and spray rates of v5-15 cc/min, Operations
Services is assisting us in this area.

Equipment has been ordered to set up ES sieve and ball mill testing
for product size evaluation.

Assistance has been given to the Flavor Development group in
determining the optimum conditions for processing DAP cased stem.
Data from the first run are currently being analyzed.

BLEND UNIFORMITY STUDIES

The major effort has been directed towards investigation of possible
methods that may be useful in determining blend uniformity.

Air separation of blend components has proved to be quite dependent
on particle size differences as well as differences in the density
between components, and therefore does not appear to be useful.

The determination of percent RCB in a blend by phosphorus analysis
is being investigated. Handblended samples containing various per-
centages of RCB in the blends have been analyzed for phosphorus,

Comments on Work Carried Out by L. M. Harwood at Philip Morris, 7/1/89 -8/ 3189

ne Extraction from Dilute Aqueous Solution by K10 Montmorillonite Clay

1, Solution strengths 0.3% nicotine were normally used, but studies were also carried out on the
tobacco extracts from the ART process. The extraction and analysis procedures are devailed in the
laboratory notebook describing my work and J. B, Paine IT is acquainted with all details ofthis
work,

2, Momimorillonite: nicotine weight ratios of 20 : 1 (ie 6.0 g clay per 100 mil. 0.3% solution) gave
95 - 98% absorption of nicotine in 15 min.

3. The nicotine may be stripped from the clay using strong base or buffer solutions at plH4.2-45.
However the most efficient stripping agent was found to be 5% HCl which removed all of the
absorbed nicotine in my hands. Stripping efficiencies compared with acid are roughly 0.1 - 0.2 for
baéfer solutions and 0.7 for alkali (N. B. J. B. Paine III has noted an apparent “curing” effect on the
clay ofthe initial acid weatmem which appears to increase its nicorine carrying capacity. This
explanation would seem to account fora series of apparently "rogue" results obtuined when
comparing the stripping capacities of various buffer solutions).

4, The atid stripping process regenerates the ability of the clay to extract nicotine. Regeneration of
perween 70 - 80% activity was noted over up to 3 cycles. Itis apparent that careful washing of the
clay is necessary in order to obtain this effect- washings should be no more acidic than pF 4 before
repeating the extraction. This is probably due to excess acid resident on the clay forming nicotine
hydrochloride.

5. None - basic materials appear not to be absarbed by the clay, as evidenced by quantitative
‘studies on an anificial solution made up of nicotine and cyclopentanone as well as studies on ART
process extract when it could be shown that only basic materials were extracted by the clay
{Subsequent G. C.-M. S, work by J.B, Paine II has identified che majority of he components
removed from ART extract by clay, showing them to be amines.).

6. Inthis work, the clay was separated from the suspension by filtration using an aspirator. When
filtering, it was preferable 1 sorape the glass sinter continuously in onder 1o prevent clogging by the
clay. This would nced 10 be taken into account if large scale fitrtion were to be carried out
possibly using close tolerance paddles of brushes bearing on tothe filter pad. Head pressure rather
than suction wouild probably be operationally more convenient on a large scale,

7. tis my opinion that large scale work would probably be better carried out by allowing the
mixture to settle and, either subjecting she slightly milky supernatant to filtration, or passing it
through another extraction stage. The clay was observed to setle out in aboot 15 - 30min when
sing 100 ml aliquots of solution. As montmorillonite clay is widely used in industrial processes,
such technical know - how should be available,

8. Some coloration of the aqueous phase was observed on the first acid wash of the clay but not on
subsequent washings - probably due to the leaching of metal cations and replacement with H". The
clay appeared to become slightly more floceolant after acid treatment but this didnot seem 10 have
any important consequence on its extractive or filwation capacities. However, it did appear to speed
up the settling process.

G.B.Schaffer and P.G.McCormick
Department of Mechanical Engineering
University of Western Australia
Nedlands, W.A., 6009 Australia

Introdue

New methods of materials synthesis, such as combustion synthesis (1] and mechanical alloying (2] have
received considerable attention in recent years. High temperature combustion synthesis involves the
spontancous formation of a compound as a self-sustaining combustion wave rapidly propagates through the
powder reactants. In general, the reaction must be highly exothermic and a critical ignition temperature is
required to initiate combustion, In contrast, mechanical alloying is a low temperature powder process. It is
carried out in a high energy ball mill where repeated ball-powder-ball and ball-powder-container collisions
resuit inthe fracture and cold welding of the powder particles. Alloying takes place by inter-diffusion across
the surfaces joined by cold welding and is assisted by high defect densities resulting from the mechanical
atwition,

A recent study [3] showed that metals snd alloys may be synthesized from their respective oxides by
mechanical alloying these oxides with a strong reducing agent. The mechanical alloying of CuO and ZnO

swith calcium resulted in che formation of Cu and Zn respectively. The co-reduction of CuO and ZnO to
bbrass was also demonstrated. In all three cases, toluene was used as a process control agent. This noie
‘escribes the occurrence of an unstable combustion reaction during the dry mechanical alloying of CuO and

<a,
Experimental Procedure

CuO and Ca powders were mechanically alloyed in a Spex 8000 mixer/mill using a hardened steel vial and
three 10mm diameter tungsten carbide balls. A 10% stoichiometric excess of calcium was used in all tests,
and the ball/powder mass ratio was 3:1. The vial was loaded and sealed in a dry nitrogen atmosphere. The
powders were milled for times up to 24h. The temperature during milling was measured using a type K
thermocouple inserted into a Imm diametes hole in the vial cover. The resulting powders were characterised
with a Rigaka X-ray diffractometer using CuKat radiation and examined in a Philips 505 SEM wi
EDAX ultra-thin window X-ray detector attatched. The ignition temperature for the reaction without
mechanical alloying was measured by embedding a thermocouple in a compacted cylinder of Ca and CuO
powders and heating the mixture in a vacuum at a tate of 20K/s until combustion occurred.

Results

Measurements of the vial temperature during mechanical alloying are shown in Fig. 1. The temperature gy
initially increased slowly with time, After approximately 6 minutes, the viel temperature increased abropily &
by 140K, followed by a relatively slow decrease. The temperature rise was virtually instantaneous,
indicating the occurrence of a combustion reaction. A change in the noise level of the mill was also aoted, 9
indicating the possibility that melting had occurred. After longer milling times, the temperature of the vial
decayed to a steady state level which was the same as (hat measured during the milling of CuO in toluene for &
similar times (3). X-ray diffraction patterns of powder laken feom the mill immediately after the temperature g
tise and after 24'h are shown in Fig. 2, Immediately after combustion, CaO and Cu were the predominant
hases present, together with unreacted CuO and a plethora of minor phases including mixed Ca-Cu oxides
and Ca-Cu intermetallic phases. The presence of CuO indicates thai not all of the contents of the vial have

have been reached by various governmental bodics and other
reviewers.

‘SUMMARY

The test material, 827, was evaluated for acute intraperitoneal
toxicity in male and female rats. Based upon the findings of this study,
‘the L050 In doth males, females and combined males and females, was

estimated to be less than 1,540 mg/kg of body weight.

INTRODUCTION

This study was designed to evaluate the acute toxicity of 627
following a single intraperitoneal dose to rats. The Range-Finding Study
was initiated on July 7, 1989 and was terminated on July 10, 1983, The

L080 study was Initiated on July 22, 1983 and was terminated on August 3,
1983.

PERIOD COVERED: MAY 13, 1977 - JUNE 10, 1977

1, COAL STRENGTH STUDIES

A. Cigarette Circumference

Cigarettes with target circumferences of 22.0, 23.0, 24.0
and 25.0 mm have been made and are being circumference selected
to #0.1 mm of the target prior to coal strength testing. The
papers used to make the cigarettes were slit to the proper widths
to maintain a constant 2 mm lap for each circumference. The data
obtained from this test will be analyzed to see how weil it fits
the formula for the bending modulus of a right circular cylinder
referenced by C. Hoelzel in a memo on coal strength dated May 10,
1977,

B. Cigarette Paper Studies

The circumference test described above was made using two
different porosity papers (16 vs. 30 second) in order to collect
additional data on the effect of paper porosity on coal strength.
Additional tests have been planned to further investigate the
relationship between cigarette paper and coal strength, These
tests will be conducted as soon as the experimental papers can be
provided by the Paper § Filler Modification group.

C. Coal Strength Test Variation

During the past month, the coal strength monitor data were
recorded according to operator to test for operator-to-operator
variation. The results showed no significant differences in the
data obtained by the three primary operators.

II. Moisturized Carbon Studies

Parliament 100 cigarettes made with standard carbon vs.
moisturized carbon in the filter have been undergoing cold storage
and shelf aging studies for four months. The gas phase reduction
data show no differences between the standard carbon and the
moisturized carbon samples after the four months of aging. The
peculiar fact about this aging study is that the filler 0.V. levels
(especially in the case of the standard carbon cigarettes} are not
decreasing with age. The decrease in filler 0.V. in production
Parliament 100 cigarettes was the reason for investigating the use

of moisturized carbon.

and defines procedures and terminology more clearly. Detailed
explanations were provided of cell culture conditions; tests for
mycoplasma contamination and karyotype stability; usage levels of
negative, positive, and solvent controls, and the 89 mixture;
solubility and dose determinations; and cell staining and scoring
techniques. The uppec dosage level of a material to be tested
was defined to ba the maximum tolerated dose, the highest dose at
which suitable nunbers of mitotic cells are obtainable. Modi~
fications were outlined for materials which are volatile or alter
pi. The major revision was the addition of a Sister Chromatid
Exchange screening assay to determine cell cycle delay. CHO
selis are exposed to various dose levels of the test compound,
with and without 59, in the presence of 5~bromedeoxyuridine?
microscopic examination of fluorescence-plus-Giemsa stained cells
after 26 hours will show if the cell cycle has been slowed down,
ensuring that in subsequent tests the CHO cells will be exposed
for an adequate amount of time, Protocol 27.1 was superceded by
Protocol 27.2 on March 22, 1984, ‘The revised protocel omitted
the section describing modifications for volatile compounds.

ABSTRACT

Immunological deficiencies have often been observed to occur in asso-
ciation with cancer although the exact nature of thia relationship

has not been fully characterized. Tha relative inmunocompetence of
individual definitely plays a major role in the ultimate suscepti~
bility or reeistance to cancer. Numerous studies support the concept
that cell-mediated immunity (CMI) is largely responsible for the body's
defense against cancer. Our laboratory is curently interested in the
levels of chemicals at which tumorigenesis occurs in various strains
of mice and whether immunocompetence of the animals is affected.

th this investigation C3H/f Mas mice ware intratracheally instilled 4
timex at weekly intervals with 500 ug of 3-methyicholanthrene dissoly-

days; wherear vehicle alone is nontoxic. Effacts on CMT were determin~

with *H-thymidine in allogenaic and spleen lyrphocyte cultures. Spleen,
thymus, and lung weight as well as blood leukocyte counts were measur—
ad. Syngeneic and allogeneic tumor transplants were performed on con-
trol and test mice co determine whether CN! data is biologically rel-
evant to the process of tumor growth. The CHI and tissue responses
were again avaluated 7, 14, and 28 days after tumor cransplantation.

Preliminary data indicates that CMI, as reflected in spleen lymphocyte
Feaponses to phytohemagglutinin, pokeweed mitogen and allogeneic anti~
gen, was suppressed during intratracheal instillations of 3-methyl-
cholanthrene. This effect was most pronounced in response to pokeweed
mitogen and persisted at least 2 weeks after exposures were discon~
sinued. Lymphocyte cultures from mica that received tumor transplants
indicate that the earlier CMI inhibition produced by carcinogenic ex-
posure is not only cancelled but actually enhanced although only
Byngeneic transplants were successful, Again, 1¢ will he of interest
to follow tha kinetics of this effect in the host and compare it ¢o
the rate of tumor transplant growch.

DIFFERENTIAL EXPOSURE MISCLASSIFICATION
IN CASE-CONTROL STUDIES
OF ENVIRONMENTAL TOBACCO SMOKE AND LUNG CANCER

Maurice Levois, Ph.D.

Paul Switzer, Ph.D.

ABSTRACT

‘We review some of the literature on the effects of exposure misclassification on the statistical
analysis of case-control studies. In particular, we focus on evidence for exposure
misclassification which may be different for cases and controls in stadies of environmentat
tobacco smoke (ETS]. For example, such misclassification could induce relative risk estimates
bbelow unity for low exposure which appears to be the case in US lung cancer and ETS studies.
‘We describe a procedures for systematically exemining the sensitivity of dose-response statistics
‘on exposure misclassification. ‘These procedures demonstrare how P-values for the null

hypothesis of no dose-response trend could be adjusted to account for exposure
misclassification. The adjustment procedures were applied to an example based on « recendly
published large study of ETTS and ung cancer in which a P-value for trend was reported #5 0.032,
In this example it is seen that modest differential exposure misclassification can induce
substantial increases in the actual P-value, changing what appears to be statistically significant to

decidedly non-significant,

Key Words: exposure misclassification, recall bias, dose-response wend, statistics, sensitivity
analysis

‘Romning Title: Differential Exposure Misclassification in Case-Control Studies

SMOKING A NOVEL SMOKING SYSTEM

August R. Buchbalter, M.A. and Thomas Eissenberg. Ph.D. Virginia
Commonwealth University

A novel smoking system (Accord®), intended to reduce second Hand
smoke. is now being marketed to smokers of “light” aad “ultra-light” cigarettes.
The system consists of'a hand-held chamber into which specially constructed
cigarettes are inserted. Puffing on the cigarette's filter, which protrudes from
the chamber. results in electronic heating of pressed tobacco within the cigareze.
Because the cigarett is mostly within the chamber. smokers do not experience
many smoking-related cues such as smoke. ash. or the buming cigarenz tip.
Due to the cigarette's construction. smokers are limited to 8 puffs. This study
examined the possibility that smokers may alter their smoking behavior when
smeking the novel system. pechaps duc to the system's absence of smoking-
related cues of limited puff availability.

‘Ten smokers who routinely smoke light or ultra-light cigarertes (> 10
cigarertes/day) will participate in this two-session. within-subject protocol.
Subjects are required to abstain from smoking for at least 8 hours before each of
two approximately 3-haur long sessions; abstinence is verified by expired CO
levels < 10 ppm. Puff topography, expired CO, vital signs. and subject-rated
messures are assessed periodically within each session as subjects smoke either
their usual brand or the novel cigarertes at 30-minute intervals for the first 2
hours and smoke ad Jib for the remaining hour. Results from the first 3 subjects
indicate that smokers" puff volume is increased when smoking the novel
cigarertes (mean = 69.2 mi) relative co their usual brand (mean = 43.5 ml). Hear
rate increased during smoking iri both conditions, though the expired air CO
boost observed after usual brand smoking (mean = 22 ppm) was not observed
with the novel system (mean =! ppm), The novel system may lead to altered
topography and appears to deliver nicotine doses with little increase in expired

carbon monoxide.

University of Rochester Medical Center
Rochester, MY 14642

Summary

A study was undertaken to determine the extent to which proteclytic
enzymes were destroying the opiate raceptor by hydrolytic cleavage
and by interaction of catalyticatly inactive forms. It wae found
that when trypsin and chymotrypsin were catalytically inactivated by
@ varlety of chentcal aud physical procedures, the inhibitory effect
on *H-dihydromorphine binding to neural membranes was about 1/3-1/4
that of the original intact enzynes, A nuwber of che peptides formed
by the autolysis of trypsin were found to be respoeible for the non-
catalytic inhib{tion of opiate binding. Included among these was
tryptic fragment 156-176, ser ala cya pro gly glu ile thr ser aon
met phe cys ala gly cyr leu glu gly gly lys, which had an ICsp of
1x lox. A number of other cryptic autolyeie peptides vere iso~
lated, characterized, and tested on opiate binding,

The fact that the opiate receptor ia destroyed by trypsin and other proteases
has led to the conclusion that the receptor is composed of a protein motety
which is essential to its stereospecific binding (1,2), With the discovery that
endogenovs peptides, euch as the enkephalins (3,4) and endorplins (5,6) possess
opfate-like activity, including ishibition of opiate binding, it vas essential
to determine whether the proteases and other inhibitory enzymes were in part
competing with the opiate ligand for the teceptor site. In an effort to clarify
this problem, studies on opiate binding vere undertaken with various inactivated
forms of proteases and other enzymes as well as with peptides derived from the
autolysis of proteasas and proteolytic cleavage of proteins.

Enzyme preparations were added 30 min at 35°C prior to the addition of [°a)-

the samples were filtered in vacuo through GF/B glasa fiber filters, washed

LICORICE ANALYSIS (G, V, Carter}

Objective: To quantitate glycyrrhizic acid in licorice
containing products.

Status: Melted Licorice block and spray dried licorice
were analyzed for glycyrrhizic acid by HPLC. The station-
ary phase was a reverse phase Cig, 5u particle size
column. The mobile phase was acetic acid in water,
gradient eluted with acetonitrile. Separation and quanti~
tation of glycyrrhizic acid in cigarette filler was also
achieved. This was done with modifications to the gradient
program,

significant differences in the nicotine and water data ford
the samples. A recommendation has been made to reduce chepe
extraction time to one hour for domestic C.1. (supplementh
only) and overseas samples. Since the history of them
nicotine and water levels for these samples is known, any

to finalize the 1991 contract with W.¥.Polytechnic University for support of
efforts to produce fillers for low sidestream papers, The contract containa
New approaches to sol-gel syntheses of mag carbonates proposed by Dr. D.
Schleich, which based on preliminary results appear to offer new opportunities
for us. This work is to be pursued by a new post-doctoral student, Aczedine
Gensalen, with whom I spent two days earlier this month in a hands on

of our methanolic and aqueous sol-gel procudures ag well as their current sol-
gel adaptations through the use of glycol solvents.

Mg(OH),+ and 15% CaCo, Multifex MM. It showed a 688 visibility reduction and
9.4 min. SBT. Pending swoking and subjective evaluationa of additional w.¥.

mag carbonate from aqueous sol-gel, consisting of 88% hydromagnesite/ 12%

high level of K, succinate flux, 9.75%. A request is being submitted for a
remake of a similar handsheet with a lower level of Eluxing agent for
reevaluation of this filler mixture.

special papez-making challenges presented by sol-gel derived fillers. In these
pursuits we have consulted with Dr, David Kraske of the University of Maine,
during his two-day visit in Richmond, Tt is felt that Dr. Kraske’a hands on
approach and expertise in papermaking should be of great benefit to us.

overcome the slow drainage and low filler retention experienced with sol-gel
derived fillers.

of the particles over the forming mat in the handsheet mold. In progress are
studies to evaluate the effect of filler particle size and filler surface
charge on filler retention during handaheet-making. The particle size sost
Likely to ba important in this aspact is that of the aggregated or flocculated
precipitate, as measured by the Malvern Particle Size Analyzer, rather than
the unit particle size, which is normally measured by SEM, and is smaller than

Gur continuing efforts to determine what controls cigarette
activity in this assay have ruggested the following tentative
conclusions based on 2 experiments per filler type or condensate
collection uethod. Bioactivity did not seem to be related to
levels of nitrate in filler! CSCs from filler with. a. high
(0,918) or a low (0.26%) nitrate content were equally active.
This equal activity reault vas also obsecved with condensates
obtained from cigarettes differing in total ‘alkaloid levels
(2.758 versus 0,518).? ‘Thus, alkaloids did not seem to be agood
predictor of biological activity in thie system. Finally, the
CSC obtained froma high nitrate filler via the cold trapped,
processed method was not different in IMC activity from CSC
iaolated from the same filler using a ary impaction trap method.!
[Other bioassays with different endpoints have shown these latter
two “collection method CSCs* to be differentially active.]
Studies with other cigarette filler types and selected CSC, fracé
tions are planned in an attempt to elucidate the nature of ciga—
rette activity in this assay.

Further work to incorporate an exogenous metabolic activa-
tion {89) system has shown that catechol was NOT active in a 1 br
treatment period.! This was in contrast to 12-O-tetradecanoyl~
phorbol-13-acetate (TPA) which was highly active in J hr, Both
of these agents were active in a 72 he treatment period." aA 1
hr treatment was initially chosen since it was shown to be com-
patible with 89 and V79 cells using another biological endpoint,
and TPA was active in this treatment time. Apparently, aI hr
treatment time is not sufficient for all chemicals to react,
especially those which are clearly positive in the "standard* 72
hr treatment period. This finding could have {mportant biolog~
ical implications as to the molecular mechanisms involved in this
assay. Subsequent experiments have shown that a°20 hr catechol
treatment produced bio-responses similar to. those obsexved for 72
hr treatments.! Other agente of known bioactivity ina 72 br
treatment are being tested for 20 hr to determine if their
response is unchanged. Shorter treatment times are essential
with an S9-mammalian cell combination since they almost always
show a direct time dependent toxicity relationship.

filter (IESF}_ electrostatic precip{tator (ESP), two 95% DOP *hospital™-type

product of the 0.3 Un particle regoval effictency and the air cleaner flow
Fate (referred to as the effectiveness factor). The IESF device was the wost
effective air cleaner, followed by {in descending order} the 95% DOP hospital-
type Filters, the 95% ASHRAE filter, the ESP, and the HEPA filter,

These studies aim ta identify the gene (or genes) responsible for active efflux
of adeianycin in the absence of P-glycoprotein, Cel? line PC4-40 represents an
example of this resistance phenotype. Our efforts are directed at isolating the
gene and identifying its preduct. The first approach we have chosen is to
transfect DNA from the resistant murine cell line into a non-mutine cell line and
isolate adriamycin-resistant mutants anong the transfected cells, The mouse DNA
can be subcloned and identified against the background of DNA of non-mutine
origin.

We cloned the FC4-40 cell Line establishing 24 independent PC4-0 cloned cell
lines. Me examined these for their uptake of adriamycin using FACS. The mutants
could be placed into chree groups based on their level of uptake. None of the cell
lines overexpressed the mdr gene(s). Cell line P0«-40 (clone 20) was selected for
our studies, since it was one of the group showing the greatesl reduction in
adrlanycin uptake as compared to the parental cell line.

The next step was to identify @ suitable cell line for transfection of the
adriamycin resistance trait from the cloned mouse PC4-40 (cl 20) cell line. We had
initially chosen the hunan Hela cell Line, but decided to test two other potential
cell lines as welt: the Chinese hamster, AUXE1 cel] line (received from V. Ling)
and the monkey Cos 7 cell line (received from P. Rosoff). The transfection method
employs plasmid pSV2-neo (received from T, Krontitis) which bears the 6418
resistance gene, This DNA will be added in excess to the genomic DNA from the PC4-
40 cell line in order to select initially transfected colls by growing che cells
in G418 after the transfection event. Subsequently the C418 resistant cells will
be subcloned Into adriamycin at appropriate selective levels. Therefore, we needed
to know the sensitivity of the potential recipient cell line to both drugs in
order to choose the appropriate cell Line for transfection.

Studies showed that the Hela cells and AUXB1 cells were 2-3 fold more sensitive
than Gos 7 cells to adriamycin. However, for all cells the amount of adriamycin
needed to sclect against the parental cells 1n a 10 or 13 day period was < 50
ng/al, within the expected range necded to select the potential adrianyein
resistant mutants. The susceptibility to 6418 of these cell lines proved more
important, The Cos 7 was the most sensitive with an ICjq of 70 ig/ml. compared to
235 ug/nl for Hela cells and > 1 mg/ml for AUXB1 cells. These results suggested
that the Cos ? cell line would be potentially most useful. Also, this {s an
already established easily transfectable cell line and can thus serve as a cell
line for latet stages of cloning of the putative efflux gene as well. If this Cos
7 cell line {s not successful, we shall try the HeLa cell Line which was more
sencitive to adriamycin, but shall need 4-fold higher levels of G418 in the
initial selection,

Plasmid pSV.neo bearing the C418 gene was transformed into an E. coli cell line
from which purified plasmid DNA has been obtained. High molecular weight DNA from
the cloned PC4-60 {at 20) cell line has been obtained using methods established.
We are presently ready to transfect the Gos 7 cell line with large nolecular-
weight genomic DNA from the PC4-40 (cell 20) cell Line along with plasmid pSV.neo
using the methods deseribed above (#11) and in our original proposal

Papillomas in mice can be induced with a variety of compounds
and these may progress to squamous cell carcinomas. These
types of studies have resulted in the basic understanding of
the multistage, multifactorial nature of cancer, In the
mouse model, a single, subthreshold dose of a carcinogen
Cnitiation) followed by repetitive application of 4
noncercinogenic agent (promotion) firet produces papillonas,
thea later squamous cell carcinomas. The mouse skin model
has been one of the best systems developed to study thie
aultistep phenomenon.

There ie mounting evidence that papillomaviruees induce
apparently benign papillomas whick, under appropriate and av
yet unknown conditions, may progress to malignant tumors.
This latter phenomenon has been observed in humans, dogs,
rabbits, and recently in mice (Cancer Cells, 5: in press).

It is possible that as animals age several factors change
which predispose the host to susceptibility to chemical
carcinogens. These may include on increase in copy number of
endogenous papillomaviruses (which appears to be the case
with Mestomve n (Nature 308:291, 1984) and harvest
mice (Cancer Cells 5: in press). In humans, epidemiologic
atudies indicate the possible interaction of several factors
in cancer of the cervix. Several specific types of human
papillomavirus DNA are conaistently found. In addition,
there is e positive correlation with smoking habits and a
specific age predilection (Obstet Gynecol Surv 39:252, 1984).

To determine the role of these factors in a mouse wodel,
tumore Will be induced on aaveral strains of mice which are
available here. These would include the epilation
heterozygote (Er/+) in which pepillomas and squanous cell
carcinomas develop spontaneously with age (INCI 75:162,
1985}, hairless aice in which a papillonavirus-like genome
hes been detected in chemically induced tumore (ASEP Ann.
Meeting, 1986), and other skin mutants which present with
lesions similar to those associated with papillomavirus
infections (such as the rhino mouse). Tumors will be induced
by single topical application of 7,12-dimethylbenzanthracene
(DMBA), followed by biweekly promotion with 12-0-
tetradecanoylphorbol-13-acetate (ZPA) (Carcinogenesis 7:949,
1986). Controle will have acetone (vehicle) applied
topically on the same schedual. Induced tumors will be
bisected, half embedded routinely for histologic
classification and screening for papillomavirus group-
specific antibodies by immunohistochemistry and the remainder

PERIOD COVERED: October 1-31, 1976

PROJECT LEADER: D. M. Teng

DATE OF REPORT: November 8, 1976

I, Chemical analyses of ten bright tobacco samples
from two different farms for the third investigation of
chemical, physical, and biochemical changes as q function
of ripeness and stalk positions were completed.!s? Determi-~
sation of filling power/expansion of these ten samples is in
progress and will be completed in the month of November.

II. In order to compile more data for the correlation
of pectin-bound calcium and filling power of tobaccg, pectin—
bound calcium from these ten samples were isolated.9 Samples
are being processed to the proper form. They will be submitted
for analysis.

III. To determine the quality and quantity of proteins in
cell wall materials, the residual proteins from tobacco samples
were isolated.4 They were hydrolyzed, and purified by an ion-
exchange column chromatographic method. The hydrolysate was
submitted for amino acid analysis by a G. C. method. The result
indicated that the hydrolysis and subsequent clesn-up procedure
for amino acids should be further defined. Work is in progress
to optimize the conditions for residual protein hydrolysis and
purification.

the proper storage conditions for potassium aitrate that has been
removed from the RL tobacco process. ‘The study is still in
progress, Proper conditions or agents to be used to keep the
potassium nitrate from molding will be determined when the study
is completed.

The equations describing combustion processes and flow processes
in burning cigarettes are now being expanded to include the
solid phase plus several species. in order to do this kinetic
expressions describing tobacco vaporization, condensation
processes, and carbon oxidation would be convenient. An

initial investigation of several tobacco weight loss curves
indicates that the gross kinetics can be described by a sum

of simple “one-step” first order kinetic functions. Presently,
the carbon oxidation and condensation data are being investiga
ted to see if a similar simplification can be made.

A set of experiments were completed that were designed to
measure dilution effects in cigarettes with conditions that
simulate a burning cigarette at high flow rates. In general,
the dilution through the paper increases with increasing flow
for Marlboro type cigarettes. However, for cigarettes with
dilution holes in the filter the total dilution decreases
slightly with increasing flow rate. The experiments will be
described in detail in a separate report.

Work has continued on the oxidation of thin foils of Ta to be
used as substrates for deposition of thin-film thermocouples.
It is possible to obtain a continuous and uniform thin film of
oxide which has a very high electrical resistance. The first
Au/Ni thin film thermocouples were deposited and calibrated
against 43 mil diameter wire thermocouple of the same
materials between room temperature and 100°C. The wire
thermocouples have a de/dT of 20u¥/°C and the thin film
thermocouples have a de/dT of 13uV/°C. Attempts witl be

made to increase de/dT by reducing the resistance.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS AND LUNG CANCER

YuZheng-feng, Li Kang, Lu Bo, Hu Tian-ming
‘and Fu Tiesshong

Harbin Medical Unive

y, Harbin, Chine

In previous epidemiological studies we observed a close relationship between smoking, passive
smoking, and lung cancer. In order to explote the role of passive smoke exposure in the etiology of lung
ances, a 1:1 matched case-control study was conducted in Heilonggiang province from October 1 of 1993
to Aptil 30 of 1994,

Materials and Methods

All of the 128 lung cancer cases collected for this investigation were confirtaed by pathologic
evaluation.

The interview questionnaire included 11 items with 64 factors such as smoking, alcohol
consumption, indoor air pollution, history of benign lung disease, dietary factors, nutritional factors,
income and general economic conditions, and family history of tumors. “The programs of “epi info” and
conditional logistic regression model were used for univariate and multivariate data analysis.

Results

In the multivariate conditional logistic regression model (adjusted for age, sex, vocation,
education level, income, and) previous years of smoking) passive smoke exposure, a major source of
indoor sir pollution, has been identified as a isk factor for lung cancer. Passive smoke exposure duting
childhood was associated with an OR of 7.8 (5% Cl: 3.1-19.8), and exposure during adulthood
associated with an OR of 2.3 (95% CL: 1.5-3.6).

FORMED FILLER BINDER

A. Qbjective: To develop a process for applying a subjectively acceptable
foamed binder ta the tobacco filler during making to improve cigarette
quality.

B. Resulte: Firmess rasults from Cambridge Lights cigarettes made with 1¢

pectin binder added to the rod at the maker show 9 4.1-0.15 mm pack
fimmess improvement over the control at standard weight. The results
also show that at this binder addition level, pack firmness numbers
equivalent to those of tha control are achieved with =24 ag less
tobacco. In order to achieve these pack firmness results, the OV of the
feed tobacco was reduced by 1.58 OV units to compensate for the
moisture increase resulting from the binder addition. Hewever, with the
Pectin costing approximately $11.26/1b, the above results do not appear
to be cost effective at this high binder addition rate, As a result,
development efforts to radesign the binder and nozzle applicator systers
are in progress to maximize the firmess improvement at the lower
addition rates (les than 0.3% add-on). Evaluation of a nozzle tip

with a reduced diameter (from 0.063" i.d. to 0.047" i.d.) looks very
Promising. Low foam densities (less than 0.15 g/cc) were achieved
without excessive pressure build-up in the foam generator. This
Pressure was minimized by telescoping down to the reduced diameter at
the final half inch of the nozzle. Testing of nozzles with diameters
raduced further is planned. objective is to further increase the foam
velocity to match or slightly exceed the suction tape speed thus
increasing the dispersion of binder within the rod.

Degraded pectin samples from Grindsted (T$-P 095) were shipped to
Australia and Switzerland for their evaluations. This pectin was shoun
to match the performance of the degraded pectins we have evaluated in-
house. The analytical data on this material along with the manual of QA
procedures was also shipped under separate cover. These results were
further discussed via telephone with FTR and Australia in more detail.

©. Blana: Redesign the binder and nozzle applicator systems and evaluate

on the Cambridge cigarette with the objective of maximizing the firmess
improvement at the lover addition rates.

Commercial quantities of dihydrocoumarin are prepared by reduction
of coumarin using a nickel catalyst under pressure at 160-200°C
or in the presence of Pd-BaS0, in alcoholic solution (1), It has
been reported to occur naturafly in Melilotus officianalis (2),
deer tongue (3), and lavender oils (4).

yoom temperature and imparts a burning taste, Table 1 shows
typical levels of use as a fragrance, while Table 2 indicates

subjective, often verbal, reports of panelists in order to obtain these kinds of
data, The problem with subjective reports js that they are very difficult to
quantify. Oftentimes you don't know what you really get when you finally get the
numbers out. For example, you have ao idea what the fnternal yardstick a
person's using represents, The verbal response “very strong" of one indtvidua}
might mean something entirely different to another individual. Sa what we're
trying to do in our research is to find measures that are objective, nonverbal
and easily quantifiable. I think the things I'm gofng to he talking about to you
today, specifically, the olfactory evoked potentials and the measures that Dr.
Koha} and Or. Silver talked about, are exactly the kinds of answers we want.
They are numerical vaTues which can be directly related to what's going an in the
nervous system and have a very good relationship to psychophysics.

Let me lay a little bit of foundation for what I'm going to discuss and tell you

the stimulus. So you can use siqnat averaging techniques to extract the response
from the brain. ‘This is a generalized recording scheme recording all types of
evoked potentials. (See Figure 2.) The essential elements here are a computer
which controls delivery of a stimulus to a subject and an electrode which fs

Statement on National Household Survey on Drug Abuse

August 21, 1996

We were not at all pleased to hear that the latest survey by the National Household
Survey on Drug Abuse (NHSDA) showed large percentage increases in 1995 among 12-
to 17-year-olds in usage of illicit drugs, marijuana, hashish and inhalants. We were
encouraged by the fact that the youth smoking rate -- whether by experimentation or
regular

smoking -- did not increase, but held constant,

We agree with Department of Health and Human Services Secretary Donna B. Shalala
and White House drvg ezar Barry McCaffrey when they say that education - by parents
and schools -- is the best solution to reduce youth experimentation with illicit drugs,
alcohol or tobacco. The increase in the rate of illicit drug use is alarming, and clearly
indicates that while the Clinton Administration has made the drug issue a top priority,
more emphasis needs to be placed in that area instead of on the Administration's proposal
to place increased regulation on tobacco -- a legal product used by 48 million adults.

Research has long indicated that peer influence, parental example and access to cigarenies
are the reasons kids begin smoking, and the Administration's proposal outlined by the
Federal Drug Administration will have a negligible effect on the youth smoking rate.

‘The tobacco industry for years has addressed these concerns with a measure of success,
Since 1991, R.J. Reynolds Tobacco bas offered and/or supported a variety of programs
aimed at curbing youth smoking. We have offered to parents brochures on how to talk to
their kids about not smoking, offered schools 4 curriculum program on handling peer
pressure to engage in inappropriate behaviors, and offered retailers a training program to
help sales personnel identify and decline sales to underage buyers of cigarettes,

‘These materials can be made available by calling 1-800-366-8441.

Functional relationship data established in the 1999 Massachusetts Benchmark Study can be
applied to estimate smoke constituent yields for other cigarettes found in the U.S. market
Mainstream smoke particulate phase constituent yields when cigarettes are smoked with the
Massachusetts smoking regimen can be most reliably estimated from either a cigarette's
nicotine or ‘tar’ yields (estimates can be based on yields measured under either FTC or
Massachusetts smoking conditions). Mainstream smoke vapor phase constituent yields can be
estimated from a cigarette’s carbon monoxide yields under either smoking condition.
Sidestream smoke constituent yields can be estimated from cigarette tobacco weight or other
general yielé parameters, A smoke constituent form that summarizes yield estimates for a
given cigarette brand style has been designed based on spreadsheet software. The constituent
farm provides information about the cigarette brand style of interest, the prediction parameters
‘employed, estimates of the individual mainstream and sidestream smoke constituent yields
predicted by interpolation fram the functional relationships established in the 1999
Massachusetts Benchmark Study and, perhaps most importantly, an indication of the range of
constituent yields expected from similar cigarettes encompassing the range of design features
found in the 1999 Massachusetts Benchmark Study cohort. Use of such a form, based on the
functional relationships developed through this “benchmarking” study, is a practical means of
providing smoke constituent data for cigarette brands sold in Massachusetts.

Quite simply, taxation is a function of the legislative branch of government, not the judicial
‘branch. To seek payment in the courts for alleged costs to the states is simply a new way to levy
a tax, State legislators have long known the risks associated with smoking and the statistical
association of smoking to certain diseases, They debate thoso issues when they determine
cigarette tax levels. They have not only condoned the sale of the product, they have also licensed
its sale, set a minimum purchase age and profited enormously, filling state coffers with high
excise taxes. Research reported by the U.S. Office of Technology Assessment, the
Congressional Research Service, Harvard University Press and the Journal of the American
Medical Association demonstrates that the current level of taxation more than covers any alleged
costs of smoking to society. Now the states want to change the rules,

As the states” chief legal officers, they are alao aware they are advocating a radical
reinterpretation of the law. IF the law as we have known it for more than 200 years is going to be

eraployee across this country. Virtually every service provided by tax dollars results from

as intended, raises the tisk of auto accidents, liver and cardio-vascular disease), purchasing
packaged goods (which, when discarded, create waste for disposs!), or eating beef or dairy
products (which, when digested, raise the risk of heart discase).

The issue here is that tobacco companies and wholesalers in these states have followed state law
to-do begal business, R.J, Reynolds Tobacco Company remeins confident that these cases have no

brain membranes with a view to identifying and eventually
isolating the specific nicotine receptors. Our approach 1,

Jocalize the specific brain regions responsible for the
discriminative properties of nicotine, i.e., tha subjective
effects of the drug in animals. Then we wiah to relate these

SUBJECT: Scientific Affairs Bvaluation of Consumer Complaint #92068

‘This complaint involves Salem ULT 85 cigarettes which were returned by a consumer
with a complaint that they ‘smelled like lighter fluid and seemed to flare up.” The cigareites
and the inside of the foil innerliner had a yellowish stain. Analysis by on-line purge-and-
rap/GC/MS and a list of compounds foreign to tobacco was generated, The compounds
identified alkanes, alkylated alkanes, cycloalkames, and naphthalene derivatives. The types
and proportions of these chemicals suggest that the cigarettes were contaminated with
hydrocarbons, such as those that might be found in kerosene or diesel fuel.

Kerosene is produced by fractionation of the middle petroleum distillate, The actual
composition of kerosene is not cbustant and differs widely, but consists mainly of a mixture
of linear and branched aliphatic hydracarbons, olefins, cycloparaffins and aromatic
compounds in the C10 to C16 range. Toxicological responses to kerosenes vary widely
according to their origin and composition. Deodorized and refined kerosenes are the least
toxic. Other kerosenes may contain benzene, alkyl benzene and other aromatic compounds
which may be toxic to the blood forming organs. The overall oral toxicity of kerosenes is
moderate, Ingestion results in rapid absorption resulting in gastrointestinal upset and
systemic effects such as drowsiness and CNS depression, Aspiration of kerosene into the
lungs may result in lung damage and even death. Kerosene is not highly volatile, and
therefore, it is not usually a hazard by inhalation. Direct contact with kerosene rapidly
defats the skin and may result irritation and inflammation. The recommended worker
exposure level for exposures 10 kerosene vapors set by NIOSH is 14 ppm. The toxicological
effects of diese! fuel are similar ta those of kerosene.

Since the consumer did not smoke these cigarettes, no adverse health effects would
be expected. Also, inhalation of the small quantity of vapors emanating from the pack or
cigarettes would not be expected to have any toxicological significance.

Dear Tournament Sponsor,

The tragic loss of Arthur Ashe has impacted those within the
tennis community as well as the scores of groups and
individuals Arthur touched during nis extraordinary life.
Arthur was well-known and well-respected as a result of his
many activities and roles =~ author, commentator, civil
xights activist, pro tennis player and ATP founder. Despite
his own tragedy, Arthur made a commitment during the last ten
months of his life to lead the fight against AIDS.

To pursue this mission, Arthur established the Arthur Ashe
Foundation for the Defeat of AIDS (AAFDA). At its inception
last summer, AAFDA embarked on a 15-month public education
and fundraising campaign. Its goal was to raise at least $5

Subject: CATEGORIES

The following are my thoughts regarding the problem of defining
tar" categories mentioned in Herb Osmon's memo of February 10.
Although Herb's memo was prompted by WINSTON Full Flavor’s plan
to reduce their tar ievel to 15 mg,, I think the issue this
raises is broader than indicated by Herb.

Before giving you my recommendation, I’d like to comment on the
alternatives Herb laid out. First, I think it would be a mistake
to classify WINSTON Full Plavor as a low tar brand simply because
its “tax” number was 15 mg. In fact, I would not favor the
adoption of any strictly numerical definition of low tar if we
are going to ise categories for planning or strategic purposes.
Thus, I am against a “sliding scale" definition also.

The problem with a strictly numerical definition of tar categories )
is basically that it doesn't fit with brand family concepts. Por
example, WINSTON King siiould not be placed in the same tar category
as WINSTON Lights regardless of their numbers. Another type of
problem is illustrated by the fact that Virginia Slims non-menthol
is currently 16 mg. while Virginia Slims Menthe] is 15 mg. In
general, “comparable” styles of the same brand family belong in the
game “tar category while lights spin-offs belong in a differant
category than their parents.

There are also a number of practical problems that will arise if

we use a "tar" category definition strictly based on a brand's
“tar” number. Category performance (sales, consumer behavior, etc.)
will be subject to discontinuous jumps, and we will have problems
comparing current/upcoming research to previous studies.

S08 [EOS

To determine how we shoula respond to the category definition pro-
blem, I think we need to consider why we have defined “tar” categories
in the first place. From my point of view, they serve two purposes.

‘The overall objective for Project FO is to evaluate the potential for the
creation of consumable new product categories for the delivery of agents which
elicit pleasure and/or satisfaction. There are two specific action steps
which address this objective. First 1s the development of unconventional
delivery systems which includes prototypes being devetoped under Projects HT
and RA, Second ts the identification of a wide range of materials which may
be el Svered to consumers that may result {n pleasure, satisfaction, or other
benefits.

Attached is a memo by Dr. Roberts and Mr. Meek which summarizes work on
the second action step. Also attached are reports by Dr. Roberts on his
discusssons with Honell staff and a literature survey by Hr. Meek on appetite
depression and satiety.

My conclusion from these and other reports is that the most attractive
area for further work {s the use of flavors/aromas to elicit a behavior
Tesponse such as stress reduction, alertness, etc. 1 recommend we establish a
research contract with Monell similar to that proposed by Dr. Roberts (10H to
D. Townsend, December 21, 1987) with the objective to identify flavors/aromas
which elicit such behavioral responses and document the actual responses with
human subjects. An HT-tike device would be the most attractive delivery
device for such a study, Results from these studies would provide additional
direction for Project FD. 1 understand Monell 1s interested in providing us 2
research proposa} for this work.

A second area of interest, although less attractive, is the delivery of
combinations of nicotine and caffeine, or nicotine and theobromine. The next
step in this area should be consultation with experts to determine expected
physiclogical effects, possibly followed by taboratory studies with animals.

mechanism of action of tumor promoters, The proposel will be based upon the

identify and predict the activity of biologically active agents. ‘This procedure has
deen used successfully in identifying and assessing a variety of biological activities
including genotoxic eareinogens, but, heretofore, hes not been used in the study of
tumor promotion, In view of the fact that it has now been realized that tumor
promotion is a widely occurring, albeit @ reversible process, and that agents active
in it may be found abundantly in the eavironment including food products and the
emissions of a variety of incomplete combustion processes, ranging from dicsols to
those of cigarettes, it would seem that any adyance in disectning the biological
basis of tumor promotion as well as in identifying tumor promoters, in @ cost
effective and highly predictive manner, would have significant impact on the
understanding of this important biological process,

‘The applicant (see enclosed C.¥.) has spent a major portion of his academic career
in developing and validating surrogate short-term tests which could be used to
identify canser-eausing chemicals, These studies have led hin to realize that with
respeet to cancer bioassays, the results in one species are not necessarily
predictive of response in another species and hence they are even less predictive of
cancer in humans, Additionally, short-term tests themselves are not infallible,
each individual assay is charactcrized by a rate of false positive and false negative
rcsponses. In order to overcome these deficiencles, we have, for the past four
years, pioneered two new approaches:

SUBJECT: Status of Pilot Project to investigate the Effect of ETS on the Air

DATE: May 9, 1986

Delivery of the briefcase sampling systems has been delayed. General
Electric, the manufacturer of the carbon monoxide (CO) monitor included in the
prototype sampling system, has decided to leave the market. This development
was unexpected by our contacts in GE's Hanufacturing Division. Consequently,
several vendors were surveyed in order to identify other CO monitors
patentiatly compatible with the prototype briefcase design. A monitor has
been identified which appears applicable, The advantages of this monitor are
smaller size and weight and easier maintenance. The monitor’s major
disadvantage is that it samples passively; thus, it will have to be modified
in order to permit active sampling. One of these monitors has been ordered on
a trial basis to confirm its applicability, Engineers responsible for
fabricating the sampling system estimate that feasibility can be assessed and
additional monitors can be ordered in approximately two weeks. In addition,
they estinate that redesign, performance tests, and fabrication will require
at most 18 weeks. Delivery of the sampling system and start of the project
are therefore expected to occur before September 1986. Purchase and delivery
of other briefcase components and supplies are on schedule.

Development of the section of the project protocol dealing with restaurant
selection is progressing wall. Restaurant. denographical information necessary
to define a representative population has been located in the trade
publication "Eating Out”, which is a monthly report prepared by the Gailup

ARE BEST FOR THE BRANDS?

We have seen a significant body of promotional ideas in the past 2 weeks for DORAL and
MAGNA from Flair, W.A. Robinson and LHC. Our task is to develop a viable short list of
good ideas which would be appropriate to discuss with consumers. (Initial groups for
DORAL are 8/28-29 and 9/6-? for MAGNA).

In.an effort to help develop that list, (since DORAL'S groups are first) we forward screening
priorities specific to DORAL, and following the priorities is a recommendation of which
presented promotions are most appropriate for DORAL. We have tried to be scrupulously
objective, applying the criteria we judge to be best for the brands, regardless of the source of
the ideas,

A similar review of MAGNA promotion ideas will be forwarded next week.
DORAL

The whole role of promotions for DORAL is dependent on where we go on price. Simply
put, if we slay where we are, trial and continuity promotions in addition to OCC's will be
necessary to compete against lower-priced brands. If we lower the price to the lowest priced
tier, price alone will be the driving force, with a possible need for continuity /loyalty-based
promotions.

We are operating from the assumption that the price of DORAL will be lowered. If Pyramid
didn’t exist, we could hold out for a while and make the move first or respond to the
inevitable competitive move. The problem is that Pyramid does exist, is growing and
squelching it will require a fairly widespread OCC offensive. By so many measures, moving
DORAL to a lower price area is nat only inevitable, it is a continued evolution of the
original DORAL point-of-difference.

The promotion ideas for DORAL in 1990 should conform to several criteria. These criteria
follow, with reasons why they are important.

Subject: SPA Menthol Positioning Strategies

This summarizes Brand/Agency thinking on positioning strategy
alternatives for a menthol product.

I. Strategic options

There are six strategic options for introducing a menthol
product:

1. The simultaneous introduction of a regular/menthol
Premier family.

2, The sequential introduction of a Premier menthol line
extension, following the successful launch of the
non-menthol product.

3. The sequential introduction of a free-standing menthol
brand, following the successful launch of Premier
(non-menthol) «

4, The lead launch of Premier as a menthol product, followed
by the non-menthol as a line extension.

5. The lead launch of a free-standing Premier menthol,
followed by the introduction of a separate {new name)
non-menthol product.

6. The lead launch of a free-standing menthol (name other
than Premier), followed by the introduction of Premier
as a (separate) non-menthol.

From a positioning standpoint:
® Options 2 and 2 utilize the same family positioning {i.e.,

"world's cleanest cigarette"), The only difference is the
timing of the menthol introduction, which is a tactical

issue. Copy adjustments (e.g., "now available in
Menthol" or “available in regular and menthol") would be
minor.

DATE: MARCH 14, 1988
FROM: D.J. DOOLITTLE ad

SUBJECT: Review of research proposal entitled "In vitro
investigations of DNA repair deficient patients with bronchial
carcinoma" submitted by Dr. H.W. Ruediger

Objective: Dr. Ruediger proposes to examine fibroblast cultures
from normal subjects and subjects with bronchial caxcinoma

for guanine-0¢-inethyl-methyltransferase (MT), a DNA repair
enzyme. :

Comments and Questions:

1) NT is but one of many enzymes which repair DNA. The only DNA
adducts that MT repairs are methyl groups on nucleotide oxygens.
Tt is incapable of repairing bulky DNA adducts resulting from,
for example, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and aromatic
amines, Why has MT been singled out as a suspect for genetic
predisposition to bronchial carcinoma? Is the author
hypothesizing that methylating agents cause bronchial
carcinoma? If 60, what are the methylating agents the author
suspects?

2) Since the target cells in bronchial carcinoma are lung
epithelial cells, why is the author proposing to look at skin
fibroblasts? Wouldnt a study on the MT activity of pulmonary
epithelial cells be more relevant? Is the activity of MT in
skin f{broblasts expected to reflect activity in lung epithelial
cells? Since MI is known to be an inducible enzyme this may not
be a good assumption to make because lung epithelial activity
may be induced by environmental exposures but skin fibroblaste,
because they would never be exposed to environmental agents,
would not be induced,

3) Many cellular enzymatic processes diminish rapidly (ca. 24
hr) following isolation and placement of the cells in culture.
How long does the author culture the skin fibroblasts before
measuring MT activity? How does subculture affect the results?
Has the author measured MT activity in fresh tissue to verify
that his observed differences are real and not an artifact from
subculturing the cells, e.g. in the half life of the enzyme? .

4) Can one be sure that the reduced MT activity observed in
tumor patients is a "genetic predisposition" and not an effect
of the tumor? or tumor treatment(see # 5)?

A dishonest campaign

The R.J. Reynolds Company is sending a van which plays

with such an aggressive promotion, R.J, Reynolds is

putting their health at risk. But what bothers us the

It is the children who will be most interested in getting
A t-shirt with a colorful cartoon character on it and it

glamorous. We don't know if the people with the van are
encouraging our youth to buy (although we wouldn't be
surprised), but it is clear that the campaign is targeted
to adults and to children.

The Surgeon General says that_smokingis:the..number one
preventable cause of death, that kills more that 1,000
people every day in the United States. some experts have
found that cigarettes are as addictive as heroin and
cocaine. Thanks to unconscientious campaigns like that
of Camel, more than 3,000 childxen begin smoking everyday.

Tt is time that we begin to protect the health of our
children and neighbors. We should call owners of business
so that they will not accept these-deadly promotions. We
should inform R.J. Reynolds that we will not accept their
selling their poison in our community.

G. R. Green reviewed the studies carried out by the Smoke Composition
Group on the ammoniation, steaming, and denicotinization of flue-cured and
burley tobaccos, the aging of burley tobaccos, anmoniated G7, and the
aminosugar levels in treated tobaccos.

duly 31, 1980,

Attendees: D. A, Colby, J, D. Fredrickson, Wayne Fulp. a. L. Harris,
G. Moser, R. M. Neel, A. J. Sensabaugh, F. A. Thome, G. W. Young

Status of the joint Analytical/Chemical study of anmoniated flue-cured
tobaccos was reviewed by J. D. Fredrickson. Most of the chemical analyses
fave been completed. The data supported previous findings on the composition
of anmoniated flue-cured tobaccos. High ammonia levels are produced by ambient
ammoniation, Ammonia levels in the samples treated with aqueous ammonia and steam
varied with the amount of ammonia added and the steam exposure. Tobacco pi varied
with the anmonia level. Nicotine reduction was primarily a function of steam
exposure, The application of ammonia prior to steaming resutted in greater nicotine
reductions. Steam exposure on the porous belt at 250°F caused @ slight reduction
in sugars, whereas anbient amnoniation resulted in large reductions in sugar
levels. Aqueous ammonia and steam on the porous belt had little effect on sugar
levels.

The ammonia-sugar reaction products determined in this study were glucosamine,
nannosamine, galactosamine, the 2,5- and 2,6-deoxyfructosazines, and the 2,5- and
2,6-fructosazines. The Tevels of these products in ammoniated flue-cured tobaccos
pay be useful indicators of aumoniation conditions. High levels of aminosugars
and fructosazines were found in the samplos treated with gaseous ammonia under
ambient conditions, Aqueous ammonia and steam on the porous belt produced much
smaller quantitics of these compounds, The effect of steam alone on the levels
of these compounds was negligible, The relative quantities of the individual
aminosugars in the anoniated tobaccos were the same as those in the untreated
tobacco (glucosamine >> mannosamine > galactosamine), The estimated quantities of
the individual fructosazines varied with ammoniation conditions, The 2,5- and 2,6-
deoxy isomers were the major fructosazines in al} ten samples.

Dear Or, Krasny:
It was indeed 8 pleasure to meet with you and Dr. Winger last Friday.

The characteristics of reduced cigarette ignition propensity listed in

your letter of May 9 were both puzzling and intriguing: Low fuel load
Seems intuitive, while high burning rate may be not so. -- However, your
personal clarifications were much useful, particularly because your im
mediate objective 1s the development of a standard method for measuring 1
the potential of different cigarettes, and not the actual measuring itself.

‘The experimental cigarettes you received from the NCI do not represent
commercial products and generalizations from their data may not be justt-
fieble. Moreover, characteristics other than paper porosity will mod{fy
combustion rates and temperatures, Significant among them is the level

of natural nitrates. which could vary from batch to batch and year to year,
thus making it difficult to rank cigarette brands, or even to develop and
Standardize an analytical procedure,

One of the important findings of the Natfonal Cancer Institute's studies
on less hazardous cigarettes is that the combustion process of cigarettes
affects the physiologic effects of smoke, and that better combustion is
Vikely to result in less hazardous smoke.

Because phystalopic effects have greater public health significance than
fire hazards of cigarettes, 1 am of the opinion that any reconmendations
toward reducing the latter should be throughly tested to insure that the
former are not adversly affected.

Such tests shovid include chemical.analys{s of smoke -» mainstream and
sidestream, particulate and gas phase -- and acute and chronic bioassay
in tissues and animals.
